request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"wesabe::fintech","name":"wesabe","one_liner":"Personal finance management website that analyzed consumer financial data and offered money-saving advice, shut down in 2010 after losing to Mint.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both businesses operate in fintech and rely on recurring revenue (subscription), but Wesabe was a B2C consumer product with a single-tier subscription, while the new pitch is B2B SaaS with per-seat pricing plus per-transaction overage tiers aimed at mid-market corporate controllers. The billing architecture and sales motion are fundamentally different."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Both operate in financial software (US, fintech), but Wesabe targeted individual consumers managing personal budgets, while the pitch targets mid-market B2B buyers (controllers, CFOs) automating the monthly accounting close. The customer segments, buying processes, and competitive landscapes are largely non-overlapping."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Wesabe grew via direct consumer sign-ups and community features (word-of-mouth, CNET coverage). The new pitch requires enterprise/mid-market B2B GTM: direct sales, accounting-firm partnerships, demo cycles, and procurement sign-off. These are entirely different motions."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both are/were early-stage web platforms in the US, pre-product-market-fit stage at founding, seeking to validate a recurring-revenue fintech model. The stage-level dynamics (burn, product iteration, investor pressure) share some structural similarity even if the customer segment differs."}},"why_similar":"Both are US-based fintech SaaS products built on recurring-revenue models, aiming to automate financial workflows for their respective customers. Both operate in a market where a dominant incumbent (Mint / large ERP vendors like NetSuite, Sage Intacct) poses a competitive threat, and both rely on the ability to ingest and process users' financial transaction data reliably. The core risk — building trust around sensitive financial data while racing a better-funded competitor — is structurally shared.","where_diverged":"The divergence is substantial across multiple concrete dimensions: (1) Customer type — Wesabe targeted individual consumers; the pitch targets mid-market enterprise controllers, a completely different buyer with procurement, security review, and contract cycles. (2) Business model — Wesabe used a flat consumer subscription; the pitch uses per-seat + per-transaction overage, a hybrid B2B pricing model that can scale with usage. (3) Product category — Wesabe was a PFM budgeting tool; the pitch automates the monthly accounting close, a mission-critical back-office workflow with high switching costs and clear ROI. (4) Sales motion — B2C viral/community-led vs. B2B direct/channel sales. (5) Competitive moat — the pitch's workflow automation creates stickier integrations (ERP, GL systems) compared to Wesabe's data-aggregation layer, which Mint replicated more cheaply.","failure_causes":["lost product differentiation to better-funded competitor (Mint)","inferior data-aggregation UX drove user churn","consumer market commoditization with low switching costs","inability to build a defensible moat before a dominant rival scaled","over-reliance on community features rather than core product quality","limited monetization leverage in free-to-enter consumer fintech market"],"lessons_for_input":["Nail the core workflow automation before layering community or advisory features — product depth is your only moat against better-funded rivals.","Secure ERP and GL integrations early to create switching costs that consumer tools like Wesabe never had.","Define a clear, measurable ROI metric (e.g., hours saved per close cycle) and make it the center of every sales conversation to justify B2B contract renewal.","Do not let a competitor replicate your data-ingestion layer; invest in proprietary integrations or data partnerships that are hard to clone.","Mid-market controllers have strict compliance and audit-trail requirements — build these in from day one to avoid costly retrofits that can stall deals."]}
